name: Gandhari
description: Gandhari, a princess of Gandhara, is a central figure in the Hindu epic Mahabharata. Known for her unwavering devotion to her husband, Dhritarashtra, the blind king of Hastinapur, she blindfolded herself for life to share his experience of darkness. This act of self-sacrifice and wifely duty is considered a testament to her virtue. Gandhari gave birth to a hundred sons, the Kauravas, whose actions ultimately led to the Kurukshetra War. Despite her sons' transgressions, Gandhari often advised them to follow dharma (righteousness). Her grief and anger at the loss of her sons during the war led her to curse Krishna, foretelling the destruction of the Yadava clan. Gandhari's character embodies strength, sacrifice, and the tragic consequences of blind loyalty and the complexities of dharma in the face of familial love and societal duty. She represents the power of a woman's will and the devastating impact of war on families.
subtitle: Devoted wife, tragic mother, righteous queen.
achievements: Maintaining unwavering devotion to her husband
Foreseeing the destruction of the Yadava clan

weapons.name: Moral Authority
weapons.description: Gandhari's pronouncements and blessings carried weight due to her reputation for truthfulness and dharma.

weapons.name: Curse
weapons.description: The power to curse individuals based on her spiritual merit and the injustice she perceived.

weapons.name: Blessings
weapons.description: Gandhari could bless individuals with protection, victory, and strength, particularly those who were righteous and just.

keyMyths.title: Blindfolding Herself
keyMyths.description: Gandhari, upon learning of her husband Dhritarashtra's blindness, voluntarily blindfolded herself to share his experience. This act symbolized her devotion and commitment to him, though some interpret it as a form of protest.

keyMyths.title: Birth of the Hundred Kauravas
keyMyths.description: Gandhari's pregnancy lasted an unusually long time. In frustration, she struck her womb, resulting in a mass of flesh that Vyasa divided into a hundred pieces. These pieces were nurtured in pots and eventually became her hundred sons, the Kauravas.

keyMyths.title: Gandhari's Curse
keyMyths.description: After witnessing the devastation of the Kurukshetra War and the death of all her sons, Gandhari, in her grief and anger, cursed Krishna. She predicted that Krishna's Yadava clan would be destroyed in a similar manner.

sources.author: John Dowson
sources.title: A Classical Dictionary of Hindu Mythology & Religion
sources.description: A comprehensive dictionary providing detailed accounts of Hindu mythological figures, including Gandhari, focusing on her role and significance in the Mahabharata.

sources.author: Irawati Karve
sources.title: Yuganta: The End of an Epoch
sources.description: A critical analysis of characters in the Mahabharata, offering insights into Gandhari's motivations and the complexities of her relationships within the epic.

associations.name: Self-Sacrifice
associations.significance: Gandhari's blindfolding symbolizes ultimate self-sacrifice for her husband.

associations.name: Wifely Devotion
associations.significance: She embodies the ideal of a devoted wife in Hindu tradition.

associations.name: Shared Experience
associations.significance: Her blindness represents sharing her husband's fate and limitations.

associations.name: Mother's Grief
associations.significance: Her curse on Krishna stems from the grief of losing all her sons.

associations.name: Spiritual Strength
associations.significance: Despite her blindness, she possesses inner strength and foresight.

associations.name: Moral Compass
associations.significance: Gandhari often speaks truth to power, acting as a moral voice.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Both characters prioritize duty and moral principles above personal safety and political expediency.
Shares a similar maternal intensity and willingness to go to great lengths for their offspring or loved ones.
Both figures are paragons of virtue, embodying ideals of marital fidelity and honor.

worshipPractices.name: Gandhari Temples
worshipPractices.description: While not as common as temples for other Mahabharata figures, some temples and shrines are dedicated to Gandhari, particularly in regions where the epic is deeply revered. Devotees seek her blessings for marital harmony, strength in adversity, and unwavering devotion.

cultural.modernLegacy: Gandhari remains a symbol of wifely devotion, sacrifice, and moral strength. Her story is often invoked in discussions about dharma (righteous conduct), the role of women in society, and the consequences of blind adherence to family loyalty. She is also a subject of academic analysis in feminist interpretations of the Mahabharata.

extendedContent.title: Early Life
extendedContent.content: Gandhari was born as a princess of Gandhara, known for her beauty and piety. Her birth name was Gandharvi. Astrological predictions suggested that her first husband would die soon after marriage. To avert this, she was symbolically married to a goat, which was then sacrificed. This technically made her a widow before marrying Dhritarashtra. When her marriage to Dhritarashtra was arranged, she learned that he was blind. In an act of profound devotion and self-sacrifice, Gandhari blindfolded herself, vowing to share his darkness and never see the world without him. This decision defined her life and cemented her reputation as a symbol of wifely duty. Her early life reveals a strong-willed woman dedicated to upholding her dharma.

extendedContent.title: Greatest Trials
extendedContent.content: Gandhari's greatest trial was undoubtedly the Kurukshetra War. As the mother of the hundred Kauravas, she witnessed the destruction of her lineage. Despite her sons' transgressions, she loved them deeply and pleaded with them to choose the path of righteousness, often advising Duryodhana against his prideful and unjust actions. Her attempts to mediate and prevent the war were largely unsuccessful. Throughout the conflict, she remained a voice of reason and morality, even as she faced the unbearable pain of losing her sons one by one. The war tested her faith, her strength, and her ability to maintain her dharma in the face of immense suffering. She was also troubled by her inability to have her pleas heard to prevent the war.

extendedContent.title: Legacy
extendedContent.content: Gandhari's legacy is complex and multifaceted. She is revered as a symbol of wifely devotion and self-sacrifice, but also criticized for her blind loyalty to her sons, which some argue contributed to the war. Her act of blindfolding herself is both admired and debated, raising questions about the nature of sacrifice and the limitations of blind adherence to duty. She is also remembered for her curse on Krishna after the war, a testament to her grief and anger at the destruction of her family. Despite the tragic events of her life, Gandhari remains a powerful and enduring figure in Hindu mythology, prompting ongoing discussions about dharma, morality, and the complexities of human relationships. Her character continues to inspire and challenge interpretations of the Mahabharata.

shortDescription: Gandhari is revered for her devotion. She blindfolded herself to share her husband's darkness, symbolizing sacrifice and duty.
